☰ Interesting Linux Contents Cheatsheet

A BookBank Field Guide · Kernel Curiosities

Interesting Linux

Eleven tricks the kernel has been hiding in plain sight —
and the syscalls that unlock them.

A field guide for curious Linux users and working software engineers — grounded in man7.org, kernel.org, and LWN.net.

The Path

What's inside

Eleven lesser-known Linux features, each explained from first principles: what it is, the problem it quietly solves, a short idiomatic example, and at least one real system that leans on it. No prior kernel experience assumed — just curiosity and a terminal.

  1. Everything Is a File Descriptoreventfd · timerfd · signalfd · pidfd
  2. Safer Ways to Open a FileO_PATH · O_TMPFILE · O_NOFOLLOW
  3. Zero-Copy Data Movementsplice · vmsplice · sendfile · tee
  4. Anonymous Memory Filesmemfd_create · file sealing
  5. Epoll's Sharp Edgesedge- vs level-triggered · EPOLLONESHOT
  6. io_uring: Async I/O Without the SyscallsSQE/CQE rings · SQPOLL
  7. Namespaces: Your Own Private LinuxPID & mount namespaces · bind mounts
  8. Kernel Tuning Secretsswappiness · THP · PSI
  9. userfaultfd: Handling Your Own Page Faultspost-copy migration · lazy restore
  10. copy_file_range: Let the Kernel Copyreflinks · server-side copy
  11. Landlock: Sandbox Yourself, No Root Neededunprivileged self-restriction
  12. CheatsheetEvery syscall & flag, one page
How to read this Turn pages with the ← → arrow keys (or the nav bar below). Tap ↑ to jump back to this Contents page. Every syscall is checked against its man page — nothing here is invented.
Contents Begin ›
⌂ Library
